title: flat-tokens data format
flat-tokens data format
text:
The flat-tokens data format is a very simple data format for storing language model training data. Unlike some other dataset libraries, it supports efficient seeking after job restarts. It also supports batch size, sequence length, and “sequence packing vs not” being selected at training time.
It is based on the simplest possible design: a concatenation of all tokens in the dataset, together with start indices of each sequence.

title: flat-tokens data format
flat-tokens data format
flat-tokens data format
Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text: Meta’s Large Concept Models replace next-token prediction with next concept-embedding prediction. The architecture has three stages: a token-to-concept encoder, a concept-level decoder, and a concept-to-token decoder. By operating on concept embeddings instead of tokens, the KV cache for the concept decoder scales as \(L / k\) instead of \(L\), where \(k\) is the concept size in tokens. A jointly-trained variant was tested and found to underperform a standard transformer at all concept sizes, including concept_size=1 where no compression occurs.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Architecture
The three stages are:

Encoder. Tokens attend to all tokens in the same block and all previous blocks (\(\leq i\)), then a reduction operation collapses each block of \(k\) tokens into a single concept embedding.
Concept decoder. Standard causal transformer over the sequence of concept embeddings.
Token decoder. Causal self-attention over tokens, plus cross-attention to concept embeddings from blocks \(< i\) (strict inequality: concept \(i\) is not available when predicting tokens in block \(i\)).

All masks are combined with segment masks to prevent attention across packed sequence boundaries. The encoder uses \(\geq\) at the block level so tokens can mix within their block before reduction; this does not leak future tokens because the cross-attention mask is strict (\(<i\)), so concept \(i\) only informs the token decoder for blocks \(i+1, i+2, \ldots\)

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Compression strategies
Four compression strategies were tested for collapsing tokens within a block:








Strategy
Formula
Description




Sum
\(c = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i\)
Element-wise sum of token representations


Max
\(c_j = \max_i\, x_{i,j}\)
Element-wise max over token representations


Learned weighted sum
\(c = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i,\quad w \in \mathbb{R}^k\) learnable
Learnable scalar weight per position within the block


Attention
\(c = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\tfrac{q^\top (XW_K)^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)\!(XW_V)W_O\)
Constant learned query \(q\) attends over projected block tokens



Implementation: forward_pass, encoder_body, concept_decoder_body, token_decoder_body.
Attention-based compression is the only strategy that appears in the top 10 experiments. Sum, max, and learned weighted sum do not. This is expected: attention-based compression is the closest to a standard transformer layer.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
What is actually compressed
The token decoder still runs full causal self-attention over all \(L\) tokens. Only the cross-attention KV cache scales as \(L/k\). The theoretical KV compression at concept size \(k\) is:
\[\frac{n_\text{self-attn} \cdot L + n_\text{cross-attn} \cdot L/k}{(n_\text{self-attn} + n_\text{cross-attn}) \cdot L}\]
At concept_size=8 with equal self-attention and cross-attention layers, this is a 56% compression in KV bytes. No memory or latency measurements were taken; the results below are loss-only.

Divergences from the LCM paper







LCM paper
This implementation




Pretrained SONAR encoder/decoder
Jointly trained from scratch


Dynamic sentence-level concepts (10–20 tokens, ≤200 chars)
Fixed-size token blocks


Diffusion-based concept decoder (best variant)
Autoregressive concept decoder only





Code
import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "plotly_mimetype+notebook_connected"

LCM_EXPERIMENTS_DIR = Path("../clearml_experiments/slim_v4_32_31m/lcm")
BASELINE_1024_DIR = Path("../clearml_experiments/slim_v4_32_31m/main/slim_v4_32_31m_lcm_baseline_b5ed44ff59864cf69c4f84d4ad2db5be")

# Window during which the encoder/cross-attention mask used `>=` and leaked within-block
# future tokens. Every block_size>1 run trained in this window beats the baseline; the fix
# (strict `>`) was in place before 2025-01-04 16:16 UTC and after 2025-01-05 06:26 UTC.
LEAKY_WINDOW = (
    datetime(2025, 1, 4, 22, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    datetime(2025, 1, 5, 6, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
)

TRACKED_FIELDS = [
    ("block_size", "Block_Size"),
    ("layers", "Concept_Decoder_Layers"),
    ("n_e_layers", "Encoder_Layers"),
    ("n_t_layers", "Token_Decoder_Layers"),
    ("reduction_strategy", "Reduction_Strategy"),
    ("n_kv", "Attention_Heads"),
    ("learning_rate", "Learning_Rate"),
    ("d_model", "d_model"),
    ("d_ff", "d_ff"),
]
# `model.layers` means concept decoder depth in LCM rows and total transformer
# depth in baseline rows; route it to a different display column per kind.
DISPLAY_KEY_OVERRIDES = {"baseline": {"layers": "Transformer_Layers"}}

# Defaults for runs that didn't override these fields. The baseline
# (configs/slim_v4-32_31m.yaml) and the LCM runs (configs/slim_v4-8_31m.yaml)
# inherit the same model section from configs/slim_base.yaml.
SHARED_DEFAULTS = {
    "d_model": 256,
    "d_ff": 1024,
    "n_kv": 16,
    "layers": 8,
    "learning_rate": 1.35e-2,
}


def calculate_ema(data, smoothing=0.97):
    ema = [data[0]]
    for value in data[1:]:
        ema.append(ema[-1] * smoothing + value * (1 - smoothing))
    return ema


def _read_csv(path):
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    return df["step"].tolist(), df["value"].tolist()


def _parse_config(metadata):
    hp = metadata.get("hyperparameters", {})
    config = {}
    for key, val in hp.items():
        if not key.startswith("Hydra/"):
            continue
        suffix = key.removeprefix("Hydra/")
        if suffix.startswith("model."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("model.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("training."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("training.")] = val
    return config


def _infer_seq_len(metadata):
    name = metadata["experiment_name"]
    if "seq_len=2048" in name or metadata.get("hyperparameters", {}).get("Hydra/training.token.len") == "2048":
        return 2048
    return 1024


def _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="lcm"):
    metadata = json.loads((exp_dir / "metadata.json").read_text())
    if _infer_seq_len(metadata) != 1024:
        return
    created = datetime.fromisoformat(metadata["created"].replace(" ", "T"))
    if kind == "lcm" and LEAKY_WINDOW[0] <= created < LEAKY_WINDOW[1]:
        kind = "leaky"
    task_id = metadata["experiment_id"]
    config_data[task_id] = _parse_config(metadata)
    loss_csv = _read_csv(exp_dir / "metrics" / "loss_loss.csv")
    if loss_csv is None:
        return
    steps, loss = loss_csv
    name = metadata["experiment_name"].replace("model.", "").removeprefix("slim_v4-32_31m_")
    metrics_data[task_id] = {
        "name": name,
        "steps": steps,
        "loss": loss,
        "final_loss": calculate_ema(loss)[-1],
        "seq_len": _infer_seq_len(metadata),
        "kind": kind,
    }


def get_metrics_data(experiments_dir, baseline_dirs=()):
    metrics_data, config_data = {}, {}
    for exp_dir in sorted(experiments_dir.iterdir()):
        if exp_dir.is_dir():
            _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="lcm")
    for exp_dir in baseline_dirs:
        _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="baseline")
    return metrics_data, config_data


def filter_metrics(metrics_data, seq_len=None, block_size=None, kinds=("lcm", "baseline"), config_data=None):
    out = {}
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if seq_len is not None and d["seq_len"] != seq_len:
            continue
        if d["kind"] not in kinds:
            continue
        if block_size is not None and d["kind"] == "lcm":
            cfg = (config_data or {}).get(guid, {})
            if str(cfg.get("block_size")) != str(block_size):
                continue
        out[guid] = d
    return out


def get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=None, ema_smoothing=0.97):
    sorted_exps = sorted(loss_data.items(), key=lambda x: calculate_ema(x[1]["loss"], smoothing=ema_smoothing)[-1])
    return dict(sorted_exps[:k] if k else sorted_exps)


def plot_loss_data(loss_data, ema_smoothing=0.97, top_k=None, opacity=0.25):
    if top_k:
        loss_data = get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=top_k, ema_smoothing=ema_smoothing)
    palette = ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd", "#8c564b",
               "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf"]
    fig = go.Figure()
    for i, (_, data) in enumerate(loss_data.items()):
        steps, loss = data["steps"], data["loss"]
        ema = calculate_ema(loss, smoothing=ema_smoothing)
        color = palette[i % len(palette)]
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=loss, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"], showlegend=False,
            line=dict(color=color, width=1), opacity=opacity, hoverinfo="skip",
        ))
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=ema, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"],
            line=dict(color=color, width=2),
            hovertemplate="%{fullData.name}<br>step=%{x}<br>loss=%{y:.4f}<extra></extra>",
        ))
    fig.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="Steps", yaxis_title="Loss",
        height=600, hovermode="closest",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.15, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=20, b=80),
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.show()


def get_eval_metrics_table(metrics_data, config_data, top_k=None):
    rows = []
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if d["final_loss"] is None:
            continue
        fields = {}
        cfg = config_data[guid]
        for config_key, display_key in TRACKED_FIELDS:
            val = cfg.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                val = SHARED_DEFAULTS.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                continue
            key = DISPLAY_KEY_OVERRIDES.get(d["kind"], {}).get(config_key, display_key)
            fields[key] = float(val) if config_key == "learning_rate" else (val if config_key == "reduction_strategy" else int(val))
        name = d["name"]
        rows.append({"Name": name if len(name) < 25 else name[:25] + "...", "Final Loss": float(d["final_loss"]), **fields})
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("Final Loss").reset_index(drop=True)
    if top_k:
        df = df.head(top_k)
    return df.style.format(precision=6).set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})


metrics_data, config_data = get_metrics_data(LCM_EXPERIMENTS_DIR, baseline_dirs=[BASELINE_1024_DIR])

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
concept_size=1 control
A 31M parameter model was trained on SlimPajama at sequence length 1024. The baseline without any concept architecture outperforms every LCM variant across the full training run.
At concept_size=1, there is no compression: each concept embedding represents exactly one token. The gap exists, which means the degradation is not caused by compression but the architecture.


Code
bs1_vs_baseline = filter_metrics(metrics_data, seq_len=1024, block_size=1, config_data=config_data)
bs1_vs_baseline.update(filter_metrics(metrics_data, seq_len=1024, kinds=("baseline",)))
plot_loss_data(bs1_vs_baseline, ema_smoothing=0.97, opacity=0.1)

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Scaling compute
The gap persists when scaling the LCM with 4–8x additional FLOPs:







Scaling axis
Values tested




Attention heads
32, 64, 128


\(d_\text{model}\)
384, 512


\(d_\text{ff}\)
\(4 \times d_\text{model}\), \(8 \times d_\text{model}\)


LCM depth
increased



Additional capacity does not close the gap. Scaling heads to 64 or 128 also nullifies KV cache savings, since the concept decoder’s own KV cache grows proportionally.


Code
seq_1024 = filter_metrics(metrics_data, seq_len=1024)
plot_loss_data(seq_1024, ema_smoothing=0.97, top_k=10, opacity=0.05)
get_eval_metrics_table(seq_1024, config_data, top_k=10)

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Limitations
Compute matching across LCM variants is approximate. The 4–8x FLOP range is a rough bound, not a controlled comparison along a single axis (parameters, training FLOPs, or inference FLOPs).
31M parameters at \(L\) = 1024 is below the regime where LCM-style methods are expected to help. The LCM paper’s claims center on long-context semantic compression. A negative result at this scale does not rule out the architecture at larger scales or longer contexts.
No experiment measures actual KV cache memory, prefill latency, or decode latency.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Next experiments
The concept_size=1 result narrows the problem to the architecture but does not identify which component is responsible. The following ablations at concept_size=1 would decompose the gap:







Ablation
What it isolates




Skip concept decoder (encoder → token decoder directly)
Cost of the concept decoder


Identity encoder (concept decoder + cross-attention, no learned encoding)
Cost of the encoder


Tied weights across all three sub-networks
Cost of joint optimization


Initialize encoder/decoder from trained baseline
Joint training vs. architecture



Other experiments in rough priority order: disable sequence packing to rule out concept boundary corruption from packed documents; increase context length to \(L\) = 4096 where the LCM thesis applies; measure actual KV cache memory and latency to plot the loss-vs-KV-bytes Pareto frontier; add an auxiliary chunk-reconstruction loss to force concept embeddings to be informative.

title: PyTree-zarr checkpoint format
text:
For seqax we write checkpoints of JAX PyTrees, in a simple format documented here.


The zarr of a PyTree is a a zarr Group with the following elements: * for each path, array in the flattened PyTree, the zarr Group contains array as a child array, with path equal to jax.tree_util.keystr(path) * additionally there is a zarr attribute by name write_completed and value True.
The zarr of a PyTree may be written to disk with any compression and chunk size settings.



We use zarr to support parallel writers from different hosts in a fully-sharded training setup. (Parallel writers in this scenario must choose a chunk size that divides the data size per host, so as to avoid zarr race conditions during writing.) Readers of the checkpoint format do not need to be aware that it was written in parallel, as this is hidden by the zarr abstraction.
We use the write_completed attribute to allow parallel writers to support a “two phase commit” protocol: all writers write their data chunks, then wait for a global barrier, then the “leader” writer sets the write_completed attribute. This protects readers from reading partially-written checkpoints.

title: PyTree-zarr checkpoint format
text:
The zarr of a PyTree is a a zarr Group with the following elements: * for each path, array in the flattened PyTree, the zarr Group contains array as a child array, with path equal to jax.tree_util.keystr(path) * additionally there is a zarr attribute by name write_completed and value True.
The zarr of a PyTree may be written to disk with any compression and chunk size settings.

title: PyTree-zarr checkpoint format
text:
We use zarr to support parallel writers from different hosts in a fully-sharded training setup. (Parallel writers in this scenario must choose a chunk size that divides the data size per host, so as to avoid zarr race conditions during writing.) Readers of the checkpoint format do not need to be aware that it was written in parallel, as this is hidden by the zarr abstraction.
We use the write_completed attribute to allow parallel writers to support a “two phase commit” protocol: all writers write their data chunks, then wait for a global barrier, then the “leader” writer sets the write_completed attribute. This protects readers from reading partially-written checkpoints.
